transcript.content:
Summarize the following news article and list its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: The three-way battle between Democrats running for Senate in Illinois has evolved into an argument about the best way to
SOURCE: New York Times (News)

ARTICLE:

The three-way battle between Democrats running for Senate in Illinois has evolved into an argument about the best way to combat President Trump’s deportation agenda.Is it better to “abolish ICE,” as Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton offers, or to “abolish Trump’s ICE,” as Representative Raja Krishnamoorthi promises?The contest’s third candidate, Representative Robin Kelly, said the country could not just abolish U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement without a plan to replace the agency’s border enforcement function, but she introduced legislation last month to impeach Kristi Noem, the Homeland Security secretary — a bill Mr. Krishnamoorthi quickly signed onto.The tenor of the Illinois race had a focus on immigration when the Trump administration sent federal agents to Chicago last fall and then shifted fully to deportation policy after Border Patrol agents shot and killed Alex Pretti in Minneapolis. The three Senate candidates, who aren’t split by major ideological or policy differences, now offer a window into how Democrats could approach dismantling Mr. Trump’s immigration enforcement structure if they win back full control in Washington.Early voting has begun for the March 17 primary race, and there is evidence in other contests that a strong anti-ICE agenda can work in low-turnout Democratic primaries. Last week in New Jersey, Analilia Mejia won a 13-candidate special primary election running as the most progressive candidate in the field on a pledge to abolish ICE. Democratic anger over Mr. Trump’s deportation policies led to a shutdown of the Department of Homeland Security when its funding expired this weekend.And yet ambitious Democrats who saw how calls to “abolish ICE” and “defund the police” hurt the party in recent years are concerned that running on a definitive message to abolish the agency is risky politics — if not in 2026 then beyond.We are having trouble retrieving the article content.Please enable JavaScript in your browser settings.Thank you for your patience while we verify access. If you are in Reader mode please exit and log into your Times account, or subscribe for all of The Times.Thank you for your patience while we verify access.Already a subscriber? Log in.Want all of The Times? Subscribe.
---

```json
{
  "summary": "Three Democratic candidates competing for a U.S. Senate seat in Illinois are focusing their campaigns on immigration enforcement and how to counter President Trump's deportation agenda. Lieutenant Governor Juliana Stratton advocates to 'abolish ICE,' Representative Raja Krishnamoorthi proposes to 'abolish Trump's ICE,' and Representative Robin Kelly opposes abolishing ICE without a replacement plan but has introduced legislation to impeach Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem. The race intensified after federal agents were sent to Chicago and following the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by Border Patrol in Minneapolis. Early voting has started for the March 17 primary, and recent wins by progressive anti-ICE candidates in low-turnout primaries suggest this messaging can be effective, though some Democrats worry about the long-term political risks of such positions.",
  "key_facts": [
    "Three Democrats are competing for a Senate seat in Illinois: Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton, Rep. Raja Krishnamoorthi, and Rep. Robin Kelly, with their campaigns centered on immigration enforcement policy.",
    "The candidates differ on ICE: Stratton wants to abolish it entirely, Krishnamoorthi wants to abolish 'Trump's ICE,' and Kelly opposes abolition without a replacement plan but introduced legislation to impeach DHS Secretary Kristi Noem.",
    "The race's focus on immigration intensified after Trump sent federal agents to Chicago and following the Border Patrol shooting death of Alex Pretti in Minneapolis.",
    "Early voting has begun for the March 17 primary election, and a recent New Jersey special primary showed anti-ICE messaging can succeed in low-turnout Democratic primaries.",
    "Some Democrats are concerned that abolish ICE rhetoric could be politically risky for the party in future elections, drawing parallels to the 'defund the police' messaging that hurt Democrats previously."
  ],
  "sources_represented": [
    "New York Times (News)"
  ]
}
```

parsed_output.summary: Three Democratic candidates competing for a U.S. Senate seat in Illinois are focusing their campaigns on immigration enforcement and how to counter President Trump's deportation agenda. Lieutenant Governor Juliana Stratton advocates to 'abolish ICE,' Representative Raja Krishnamoorthi proposes to 'abolish Trump's ICE,' and Representative Robin Kelly opposes abolishing ICE without a replacement plan but has introduced legislation to impeach Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem. The race intensified after federal agents were sent to Chicago and following the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by Border Patrol in Minneapolis. Early voting has started for the March 17 primary, and recent wins by progressive anti-ICE candidates in low-turnout primaries suggest this messaging can be effective, though some Democrats worry about the long-term political risks of such positions.
key_facts: Three Democrats are competing for a Senate seat in Illinois: Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton, Rep. Raja Krishnamoorthi, and Rep. Robin Kelly, with their campaigns centered on immigration enforcement policy.

The candidates differ on ICE: Stratton wants to abolish it entirely, Krishnamoorthi wants to abolish 'Trump's ICE,' and Kelly opposes abolition without a replacement plan but introduced legislation to impeach DHS Secretary Kristi Noem.
The race's focus on immigration intensified after Trump sent federal agents to Chicago and following the Border Patrol shooting death of Alex Pretti in Minneapolis.
Early voting has begun for the March 17 primary election, and a recent New Jersey special primary showed anti-ICE messaging can succeed in low-turnout Democratic primaries.

Some Democrats are concerned that abolish ICE rhetoric could be politically risky for the party in future elections, drawing parallels to the 'defund the police' messaging that hurt Democrats previously.
